Title: Defective Angiogenesis in the Inflammatory Granulation Tissue in Histidine Decarboxylase–deficient Mice but not in Mast Cell–deficient Mice

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes VEGF production and angiogenesis in inflammatory granulation tissue; relevant to wound healing and ischemic disease responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Granulation tissue and surrounding implanted tissue
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in infiltrating macrophages in granulation tissue after cotton thread implantation; tissue HDC activity increased from day 1 to 5
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: HDC-producing cells detected mainly in infiltrating macrophages of granulation tissue; activity low/absent in Hdc-/- mice and induced even in mast cell-deficient tissue
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cotton thread implantation; histamine or dimaprit treatment; cimetidine inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for endogenous histamine production in this model
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of Hdc reduces histamine production, lowers VEGF levels, impairs angiogenesis, and decreases granulation tissue formation; restoration of histamine signaling rescues the phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene knockout (Hdc-/-); rescue with histamine and H2 agonist dimaprit; HDC activity assay; immunohistochemistry; VEGF immunoblot/ELISA; angiogenesis and granulation tissue phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pharmacological target to modulate histamine-dependent angiogenesis; increasing HDC/histamine signaling may support wound healing, whereas suppression may limit pathological granulation or tumor-associated angiogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhancing histamine production may aggravate inflammatory responses or pathological angiogenesis; inhibition may delay wound healing
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study shows histamine driving angiogenesis is mainly derived from nonmast cells, especially infiltrating macrophages, rather than mast cells
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Hdc deficiency caused reduced VEGF accumulation, reduced neovascularization, and reduced granulation tissue formation after cotton thread implantation. 2. Exogenous histamine or the H2 receptor agonist dimaprit rescued the angiogenesis defect in Hdc-deficient mice. 3. HDC activity was induced in implanted tissue and HDC-positive cells in granulation tissue were mainly infiltrating macrophages, supporting nonmast-cell histamine production.
